title: OpenAI and Broadcom unveil LLM-optimized inference chip
blurb: OpenAI and Broadcom introduced Jalapeño, a custom chip built specifically for LLM inference rather than training. The companies say the design targets higher performance and efficiency at scale, extending OpenAI's push to control its own compute stack and reduce dependence on Nvidia GPUs for serving its models.

title: OpenAI and Broadcom unveil Jalapeño, an LLM-optimized inference chip developed from design to manufacturing tape-out in nine months, aided by OpenAI's models (OpenAI)
blurb: OpenAI and Broadcom introduced Jalapeño, an inference chip optimized for large language models that went from design to manufacturing tape-out in nine months. OpenAI used its own models to accelerate the chip design process, signaling a push to control its hardware stack and cut reliance on Nvidia GPUs for serving inference at scale.

title: OpenAI unveils its first custom chip, built by Broadcom
blurb: OpenAI released Jalapeño, its first custom inference chip, designed with Broadcom to cut the company's reliance on Nvidia GPUs. Still in testing, the processor targets real-time workloads like coding models and reportedly shows significantly better performance-per-watt than current alternatives, joining Google and Amazon in building in-house silicon to lower the cost of running AI at scale.

title: Embedding Forbidden Text in Spyware to Discourage AI Analysis
blurb: Malware authors are embedding fake instructions about nuclear and biological weapons into spyware code comments to derail AI-based security analysis. The text sits in a JavaScript comment that runtimes skip, but it can trigger refusals, prompt confusion, or premature classification in LLM-first triage systems that feed raw file headers to a model without isolating them as untrusted data.

title: Zero-Day Exploitation of Vulnerability (CVE-2026-20245) in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager
blurb: Mandiant identified a threat actor exploiting a zero-day vulnerability (CVE-2026-20245) in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager to escalate a compromised admin account to root via a malicious CSV upload. The attacker first gained access through rogue peering connections, then ran extensive anti-forensic cleanup, deleting files and reverting configuration changes to purge indicators of the intrusion.

title: OpenAI, Broadcom Unveil Chip to Run Models Faster, Cheaper
blurb: OpenAI introduced its first custom AI chip, codenamed Jalapeno, developed with Broadcom to run its models faster and at lower cost. OpenAI has received initial samples and is testing them on real workloads, with Broadcom CEO Hock Tan citing roughly 50% cost savings versus standard AI GPUs, part of OpenAI's push to reduce its dependence on Nvidia.

title: OpenAI reveals its first AI processor: Jalapeño
blurb: OpenAI introduced Jalapeño, its first custom AI chip, built with Broadcom to run inference for products like ChatGPT and Codex and cut its reliance on scarce Nvidia GPUs. Broadcom's CEO says the ASIC matches Nvidia's Blackwell and Google's TPUs, and OpenAI calls it the first step in a multi-generation compute platform it expects to deploy by the end of 2026.

title: Cordyceps CI/CD Flaws Expose 300+ GitHub Repositories to Supply-Chain Attacks
blurb: Researchers at Novee Security disclosed Cordyceps, a class of CI/CD misconfiguration that lets any unauthenticated user with a free account forge approvals, push code, or steal credentials by exploiting workflows that grant untrusted pull requests excessive permissions. A scan of roughly 30,000 high-impact repositories found more than 300 fully exploitable, including projects at Microsoft, Google, Apache, and Cloudflare, where the flaw enables code execution, token theft, and supply-chain compromise.

title: OpenAI, Broadcom Develop AI Chip Called 'Jalapeno'
blurb: OpenAI and Broadcom have developed a custom AI chip called Jalapeno, with OpenAI now testing early samples. The companies say the chip can cut costs by 50%, advancing OpenAI's push to reduce its dependence on Nvidia by designing silicon tuned to its own workloads.

title: Exploitable CI/CD Vulnerabilities Expose Millions of Repositories to Hijacking
blurb: Security firm Novee disclosed Cordyceps, a class of CI/CD vulnerabilities in GitHub Actions YAML that lets unauthenticated attackers with free accounts hijack developer workflows and seize control of millions of repositories. A single scan flagged 654 repositories and confirmed over 300 fully exploitable, affecting build tooling from Microsoft, Google, Apache, Cloudflare, and the Python Software Foundation, with insecure patterns spread further by agentic coding.

title: AI Is Designing Radio Chips That Humans Couldn’t Even Imagine
blurb: Princeton researchers trained reinforcement learning and diffusion models to design radio-frequency integrated circuits from scratch, producing record-performance layouts that abandon the human-readable symmetry RFIC engineers rely on. The approach compresses a design process that normally takes expert engineers months into far less time, and could accelerate the RF chips underpinning 5G, satellite links, and autonomous vehicles.

title: OpenAI, Broadcom Develop Custom Chip for AI Inference
blurb: OpenAI and Broadcom developed a custom AI inference chip called Jalapeño, built to improve efficiency and cut the cost of running OpenAI's models. The chip advances OpenAI's push to own more of its stack, reducing its reliance on Nvidia GPUs as inference demand from ChatGPT and its API continues to climb.

title: Boffin claims Microsoft's supposed quantum leap does not compute due to 'basic Python errors'
blurb: A peer-reviewed critique in Nature argues that Microsoft's 2025 Majorana quantum claims rest on flawed tune-up software and coding errors that produced incorrect statements to reviewers. Author Henry Legg of the University of St Andrews says omitted raw data points to device disorder incompatible with a topological gap, and estimates Microsoft is centuries, not years, from a working topological quantum computer.

title: Qualcomm to Buy Modular for $3.9 Billion to Help AI Push
blurb: Qualcomm agreed to acquire AI software startup Modular for about $3.9B in stock, gaining inference and compiler technology to support its expansion into data centers. The all-stock deal hands Modular's owners 19.2 million Qualcomm shares and is expected to close in the second half of 2026, deepening the chipmaker's push beyond mobile into AI infrastructure.

title: Krea 2 Technical Report
blurb: Krea 2 is a series of foundation image-generation models built for creative exploration across styles rather than a single default aesthetic.

title: Attackers exploit Cisco Unified CM flaw weeks after patch release
blurb: Attackers are now exploiting Cisco Unified CM's critical SSRF flaw (CVE-2026-20230), weeks after Cisco's June 3 patch warned it could grant root access.

title: Apple's MacOS Gap Lets Users Disable Security Tools
blurb: Researchers found a macOS technique that lets standard users disable EDR and MDM tools without admin credentials or kernel exploits.

title: Microsoft and Allies Smash Shared Infrastructure of Amadey and StealC Malware
blurb: Microsoft and partners disrupted shared C&C infrastructure behind the Amadey loader and StealC infostealer, seizing 25M stolen credentials under Operation Endgame.

title: Medical diagnosis AIs can be tricked into telling whose data trained them
blurb: Researchers showed that medical diagnosis models can be manipulated into revealing whose data trained them, exposing patients through membership inference attacks. The finding highlights a privacy gap in healthcare AI, where models built on sensitive records can leak whether a specific individual was part of the training set.

title: Qualcomm says it will acquire Modular, which builds a chip software platform and has a proprietary coding language, in a nearly $4B deal set to close in H2 2026 (Lauren Goode/Wired)
blurb: Qualcomm will acquire Modular, the AI infrastructure startup behind a chip software platform and the Mojo programming language, in a deal worth nearly $4B expected to close in H2 2026. The purchase gives Qualcomm an in-house software stack to compete with Nvidia's CUDA, broadening its push beyond mobile chips into AI data center and inference workloads.

title: White House app auto-downloads to government phones, can't be uninstalled
blurb: The White House app now auto-downloads onto the work phones of millions of federal employees and reinstalls itself when deleted, according to workers at the USDA, State Department, and Department of Labor. The app surfaces curated pro-administration news, Trump's Truth Social posts, and a "text President Trump" button, while its App Store privacy policy links to a page with no details on the app's own data handling.

title: Adobe Just Deferred a Big Annual Price Increase. It’s the First Big Crack in B2B Pricing Power Since 2022.
blurb: Adobe told Wall Street on its Q2 FY2026 call that it is deferring roughly $500M in planned Creative Cloud price increases, breaking a decade-long pattern of clockwork annual hikes. The move came alongside record revenue of $6.62B, up 13% year over year, but the stock is down about 38% year to date as investors read the deferral as the first crack in B2B pricing power since 2022.

title: SK hynix files to raise up to $29 billion in historic Nasdaq listing — all proceeds going to advanced AI memory fabs and EUV tool orders
blurb: SK hynix filed to raise up to $29.43B through a Nasdaq ADR listing set for July 10th, with all proceeds funding new memory fabs, advanced packaging, and EUV tool orders. The money targets its Y1 fab in Yongin and a Cheongju HBM packaging plant, neither of which reaches volume output until 2027, meaning the raise does nothing to ease the DRAM and HBM shortage still pushing prices up.

title: ‘Digit’ maker Agility Robotics to go public in $2.5B deal — here’s what the filings say about its finances
blurb: Agility Robotics, the Oregon maker of the two-legged Digit warehouse robot, is going public through a SPAC merger with Churchill Capital Corp XI that values it at $2.5B and provides more than $620M in cash. The deal makes Agility the first US public company focused solely on humanoid robots, though early filings withhold revenue and show it burned roughly $100M in 2025 while remaining unprofitable.

title: We're making Bunny DNS free: because a faster internet won't build itself
blurb: Bunny.net eliminated all DNS query fees, making Bunny DNS free with hosting for up to 500 domains per account and no query limits or per-request billing. The service, which grew from an internal CDN routing engine into a product handling nearly 200 billion queries monthly across 300,000 domains, supports latency-based routing, health checks, and JavaScript-driven request logic.

title: HPE’s Slingshot tops supercomputer interconnects, but China steals the show
blurb: HPE's Slingshot 11 fabric became the most common interconnect on the TOP500 supercomputer list, edging past 100G Ethernet for the first time with 57 systems (11.4%) versus 56. The proprietary Cray-developed technology now powers four of the top five systems, including Frontier and Aurora, though a mysterious Chinese machine running an unknown fabric unseated El Capitan from the overall number-one spot.

title: Founding a company in Germany: €9600, 152 days and I still can't send an invoice
blurb: Founding a UG & Co. KG in Germany cost this engineer €9,600 and 152 days, yet five months in he still can't send a single invoice because his VAT ID hasn't arrived. The author details a timeline where a law firm, notary, two courts, a tax firm, and software vendors all billed him on schedule, while the one function the state exists to enable, clean invoicing, remains blocked.

title: Google tests voice dictation and Magic Pointer on Gemini desktop
blurb: Google is testing system-wide voice dictation and a cursor-tracking Magic Pointer in the macOS Gemini desktop app, alongside early signs of cross-device linking. The features would let Gemini act on whatever a user is doing across apps rather than inside a single window, pushing the assistant toward the always-on desktop agent role Microsoft and OpenAI are also chasing.

title: TSMC is reportedly hiking prices for 'all advanced nodes,' accounting for 74% of the company’s wafer business — Nvidia, AMD, Apple, Qualcomm, and others will face higher wafer costs
blurb: TSMC has reportedly told customers to prepare for price increases across all advanced nodes, extending hikes beyond 3nm to include 5nm and 7nm processes that together account for 74% of its wafer revenue. The reported 5% to 10% increases would raise wafer costs for Apple, Nvidia, AMD, Qualcomm, Broadcom, and MediaTek, with some already rolling out and others folded into future purchase orders.

title: China black market Nvidia prices rocket in wake of smuggling crackdown and customs freeze — five-year-old A100 servers triple in price, now fetching up to $82,000
blurb: Black-market prices for Nvidia AI hardware in China have spiked as a US smuggling crackdown and a Chinese customs freeze cut off every supply route at once, with five-year-old A100 servers tripling to roughly $82,000 and DGX B300 systems doubling to over $1.1 million. With smuggled and legal channels both choked and rental rates now matching US prices, the squeeze pushes Chinese buyers toward domestic alternatives like Huawei's Ascend line.

title: RunPod, which rents access to non-Nvidia servers, raised $100M led by Summit Partners at a $1B valuation, a source says up from $100M after its seed in 2024 (Stephanie Palazzolo/The Information)
blurb: RunPod, a cloud provider that rents GPU and non-Nvidia compute to AI developers, raised $100M led by Summit Partners at a $1B valuation. The round vaults the company to unicorn status roughly two years after its 2024 seed, betting that demand for cheaper alternatives to Nvidia-dominated infrastructure will keep climbing as startups hunt for affordable training and inference capacity.

title: SpaceX launches secretive Starfall reentry demo mission
blurb: SpaceX launched the first test flight of its Starfall reentry capsule on June 23, a vehicle the company describes as a microgravity lab for in-space manufacturing and point-to-point cargo delivery. SpaceX ran the flight like a classified mission, withholding upper-stage video and confirming only that Starfall deployed, without saying whether the 2,100-kilogram spacecraft survived its Pacific reentry and splashdown.

title: Slate Auto’s radically simple electric truck starts at $24,950
blurb: Slate Auto, the EV startup backed by Jeff Bezos, set the starting price of its electric pickup at $24,950 and opened preorders, roughly half the average cost of a new US car. The bare two-seater ships with hand-crank windows and no infotainment, converts into a five-seater SUV starting at $29,950, and undercuts rivals like the $29,000 Chevrolet Bolt.

title: Google’s New $100 Speaker Makes a Weak Case for Gemini in the Home
blurb: Google released the Google Home Speaker, a $100 device launching June 25 that runs Gemini for always-on voice interaction and marks Alphabet's first new smart speaker in six years. Reviewers found the AI capable but the audio quality underwhelming, undercutting Google's pitch to replace Assistant-era hardware and bring Gemini into the home as a standalone product.

title: Microsoft's Digital Crimes Unit says AI helped it link two separate hacking tools, Amadey and StealC, and file a single civil lawsuit to help take them down (Lorelei Smillie/Bloomberg)
blurb: Microsoft's Digital Crimes Unit used AI to link two previously separate malware operations, Amadey and StealC, and consolidate them into a single civil lawsuit aimed at dismantling both. The AI surfaced connections across the tools' infrastructure that let the unit pursue one coordinated takedown rather than chasing each threat independently.

title: Qualcomm inks deal for AI startup Modular to bolster software stack, data center build-out
blurb: Qualcomm acquired AI infrastructure startup Modular for a reported $3.92B to strengthen its software stack and push deeper into the data center inference market. The deal, expected to close in the second half of 2026, gives Qualcomm a developer-friendly platform for running AI across diverse compute environments as enterprises hunt for ways to cut soaring token costs.

title: Chinese Supercomputer Overtakes U.S. as World’s Fastest
blurb: China's LineShine system took the No. 1 spot on the latest Top500 supercomputer ranking released this week, displacing U.S. machines from the top of the list. The result marks China's return to the public ranking after years of withholding submissions, signaling intensifying competition over high-performance computing capacity that underpins AI and scientific research.

title: Walmart buys Vibe.co for $1.4bn to chase Amazon in ads
blurb: Walmart agreed to acquire Vibe.co, a self-serve connected-TV advertising platform for small and mid-sized businesses, in a deal two reports put at $1.4B, the retailer's biggest acquisition in two years. The price values Vibe.co at roughly 12.6 times revenue, more than triple its $410M valuation last September, signaling how aggressively retailers are spending to build out high-margin ad businesses and catch Amazon.

title: Memory Chipmaker SK Hynix's $29 Billion US Listing Seizes on AI Demand
blurb: SK Hynix is seeking $29.4 billion in a US listing of American depositary receipts, tapping investor appetite for memory-chip stocks tied to AI demand. The offering follows an 850% climb in the company's Seoul-traded shares over the past 12 months, which pushed its market value above $1 trillion despite a sharp selloff that hit the sector this week.

title: MGX Sets Up $50 Billion AI Fund
blurb: Abu Dhabi's MGX raised close to $50B from regional and global investors, creating one of the largest dedicated AI investment vehicles to date. The state-backed fund adds to the Gulf's growing push to finance frontier AI infrastructure and startups, joining sovereign players like Saudi Arabia's PIF in steering capital toward the sector.

title: South Korea’s biggest chipmaker SK Hynix plans to raise $29 billion via Nasdaq listing as soon as July 10
blurb: SK Hynix plans to raise around $29B by issuing American depositary receipts on the Nasdaq, with trading tentatively set to begin July 10. The South Korean memory giant, which holds roughly 60% of the HBM market and has seen shares climb over 280% this year, is funding aggressive expansion to meet AI-driven demand, including a new fab cluster at home and a $4B packaging plant in Indiana.

title: The VC that turned a $750M Anthropic deal into a $14B stake just raised $3B to do it again
blurb: Menlo Ventures raised $3 billion across two funds, an early-stage vehicle and a growth fund for Series B and later companies, its largest raise in 50 years. The firm's roughly $1 billion bet on Anthropic since 2023 is now worth about $14 billion, the return that anchored this raise and funds Menlo's push across the full AI stack.

title: Agility Robotics goes public at a $2.5B valuation, and its humanoid robots are already working in warehouses
blurb: Agility Robotics is going public via a SPAC merger with Churchill Capital Corp XI at a $2.5B valuation, with more than $620M in expected gross proceeds. Its Digit humanoid already runs on live production floors at Schaeffler, GXO, Toyota, and Mercado Libre, logging over 65,000 operating hours across nine facilities, and iPhone manufacturer Foxconn is leading the PIPE investment.

title: Metas Ex-KI-Chef sammelt über eine Milliarde Dollar für neues Startup ein
blurb: Yann LeCun's new Paris-based startup AMI Labs raised $1.03B in seed funding, backed by Bezos Expeditions, Greycroft, HV Capital, Cathay Innovation, and Hiro Capital. The venture, which LeCun launched after leaving Meta following 12 years leading its AI research, will focus on world models and has named former Facebook researcher Alex LeBrun as CEO.

title: Meta, Microsoft Lead $850 Billion Boom in Data Center Leases
blurb: Meta and Microsoft each committed tens of billions of dollars in new data center leases last quarter, pushing the largest cloud providers' total future lease obligations past $850 billion. The figure, drawn from a Bloomberg analysis of regulatory filings, has climbed steadily over the past year as tech giants race to expand their server farms for AI workloads.

title: SK Hynix Seeks to Raise $29 Billion in US Listing to Meet AI Chip Demand
blurb: SK Hynix filed to raise $29.4B in a US listing expected to begin trading July 10, a deal that could rank among the five largest share sales ever. The South Korean memory maker is tapping public markets to fund expansion of HBM production, the high-bandwidth chips that pair with Nvidia GPUs and have become a critical bottleneck for AI compute.

title: Microsoft Says Copilot AI Helped Knock Down Cybercrime Tools
blurb: Microsoft said its Copilot AI helped investigators dismantle long-running cybercrime tools, using new AI capabilities to defeat older malware infrastructure. The disclosure positions Copilot as an offensive security asset for Microsoft, extending the assistant's role from productivity into active threat takedowns alongside the company's existing security operations.

title: Zhipu Said to Weigh Multibillion-Dollar Share Sale in Hong Kong After 2,000% Gain
blurb: Chinese AI model maker Zhipu is weighing a Hong Kong share sale to raise several billion dollars, people familiar with the matter told Bloomberg, after its stock climbed roughly 2,000% since listing in January. The placement could come as soon as next month, following the July 8 expiry of a six-month IPO lock-up.

title: A Princeton grad’s $30M AI detector is selling to Superhuman
blurb: Superhuman, the productivity company formerly known as Grammarly, acquired GPTZero, the AI-detection startup founded by Princeton grad Edward Tian, for undisclosed terms. GPTZero passed 19 million registered users and $30M in annual recurring revenue on just $13.5M raised, and its 30-person team will now build what Superhuman calls an "authenticity layer" for proving content is human-made.

title: SpaceX Starfall Demo Provides Validation for Reentry Startups
blurb: SpaceX launched a demo of its Starfall reentry capsule, entering a market already contested by about a dozen startups building return vehicles for in-orbit manufacturing. Starfall carries roughly 1 metric ton and is about 3x the size of Varda's capsules, but lacks in-orbit maneuverability and precise landing guidance, leading rival founders to read the move as validation of demand rather than a threat.

title: Minimus container images are now free
blurb: Minimus now offers its hardened, security-configured container images free, deployable via curated Helm charts, with no support or guaranteed patching on the free tier.

title: Microsoft rivals line up to tell UK watchdog where the software behemoth hurt them
blurb: Microsoft's competitors are submitting complaints to the UK's Competition and Markets Authority, detailing how the company's cloud licensing practices have disadvantaged them in the market. The testimony feeds an ongoing CMA investigation into whether Microsoft uses Windows Server and Office licensing terms to steer customers toward Azure, a probe that could force changes to how the company prices software across rival clouds.

title: Home Broadband Is 5G’s Surprise Killer App
blurb: Fixed wireless access, which delivers home broadband over 5G cellular towers instead of cable or fiber, now serves over 14 million U.S. customers and carries 28% of worldwide wireless traffic. Carriers repurpose surplus 5G capacity and a usage quirk, mobile traffic falls after 8 p.m. as home demand peaks, to undercut fiber deployment costs and reach rural areas more cheaply than satellite services like Starlink.

title: Make an Origami Circuit Board
blurb: Researchers at City University of Hong Kong's MEI Lab developed a method to create circuit traces in paper by folding and cutting it, using a nonwoven textile impregnated with gallium-indium liquid metal. The pressure from origami and kirigami techniques, ranging from 2.5 to 100 megapascals, cracks an insulating oxide layer to form conductive paths, letting crafters embed LEDs and motors without adhesive copper tape or shaped molds.

title: Google Home Speaker review: the $100 speaker, the company's first such device in six years, works for Gemini power users but has underwhelming music playback (Chris Welch/Bloomberg)
blurb: Google released the $100 Google Home Speaker, its first smart speaker in six years and a hub for Gemini voice interactions across the home. Reviewers found it capable for Gemini power users but weak on music playback, leaving it short of dedicated audio hardware despite the upgraded assistant.

title: AI-Powered Emails Are Influencing Politics on Hot-Button Issues
blurb: AI-powered platforms are flooding government public-comment systems with mass-generated emails, prompting officials in California, North Carolina, and elsewhere to question whether the messages reflect real constituents. A Southern California air-quality agency weighing a gas-appliance phase-out received thousands of AI-generated opposition emails, forcing staff to investigate the campaign's origin and undermining a process meant to gauge genuine public sentiment.

title: Slate Auto says each $24,950 electric pickup truck will be profitable as it aims to be cash-flow positive next year
blurb: Slate Auto says every $24,950 electric pickup it builds will be gross-margin positive, targeting positive free cash flow and EBITDA by 2027. The Bezos- and Mark Walter-backed startup is betting a bare-bones, highly customizable truck and an 80,000-unit annual break-even can succeed where Rivian, Lucid, Fisker, and Lordstown have piled up losses or gone bankrupt.

title: US government reportedly urging Meta to share its AI models
blurb: The US government is reportedly urging Meta to submit its AI models for safety and security review, making it the only major AI developer that hasn't voluntarily done so, according to The New York Times. OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, xAI, and Microsoft are already sharing models with the Center for AI Standards and Innovation, following a June 2 executive order that gives authorities up to 30 days to evaluate releases before launch.

title: Nvidia and Samsung to power 6G-ready digital twin for Japan telecom giant
blurb: KDDI launched a joint initiative with Nvidia, Samsung, and Keysight to build a high-fidelity digital twin of its radio access network for the 6G era. The virtual replica lets operators train and validate AI optimization models against simulated radio conditions without touching live networks, with a prototype targeted for March 2028 and broader rollout by 2030.

title: Hadrian’s reported $7.5bn round and the physical-AI rush
blurb: Hadrian Automation is reportedly in talks to raise up to $1bn at a $7.5bn valuation, more than quadrupling the $1.6bn mark it set in January, though the company told Bloomberg the figures are "inaccurate." The defense-factory startup builds AI-run plants for aerospace and Navy parts, and the disputed markup tracks how fast capital is chasing physical AI and US reindustrialization.

title: The US FDA drops an enforcement complaint against Whoop over its blood pressure tracking tool, reversing a July 2025 warning letter; Whoop is modifying the tool (Samantha Kelly/Bloomberg)
blurb: The FDA dropped its enforcement complaint against Whoop over the blood pressure tracking feature on its wearables, reversing a July 2025 warning letter that argued the tool required medical-device clearance. Whoop is modifying the feature as part of the resolution, a sign regulators are still working out how consumer wearables can offer health metrics without crossing into regulated diagnostic territory.

title: Q&A with AWS CEO Matt Garman on the parallels between early AWS and AI, Quick, AI coding, Amazon's $200B capex in 2026, entry-level jobs changing, and more (Casey Newton/Platformer)
blurb: In a Platformer Q&A, AWS CEO Matt Garman drew parallels between today's AI buildout and the early days of cloud, framing both as infrastructure bets that looked oversized before demand caught up. He confirmed Amazon plans roughly $200B in capex in 2026 and addressed how AI coding tools are reshaping entry-level engineering roles.

title: SK Hynix sets a $29 billion US listing that could top every ADR deal ever done
blurb: SK Hynix filed to raise up to $29B through a Nasdaq listing of American depositary receipts, which would top Alibaba's $21.8B 2014 debut as the largest ADR sale ever, with trading set to begin July 10. The entire raise will fund memory capacity, including a Yongin fab and EUV scanners, as the company leans on its dominant high-bandwidth memory position supplying Nvidia's accelerators.

title: York satellite demonstrates two-way UHF communications from low Earth orbit
blurb: York Space Systems' Dragoon satellite demonstrated two-way UHF tactical communications from low Earth orbit, completing five downlink and uplink tests over three months for the Space Force's Space Development Agency. The experiment tests whether UHF services traditionally delivered by geostationary satellites can move to smaller, closer spacecraft, and marks the first in-orbit run of York's larger LX-class platform.

title: Beyond the $7.8B in deals: Why Wall Street is suddenly watching Argentum AI
blurb: Argentum AI, the year-old infrastructure startup led by Andrew Sobko and backed by Supermicro, signed roughly $7.8B in agreements to deploy about 47,000 Nvidia GB300 GPUs across a 300-megawatt datacenter buildout. Unlike rivals who buy GPUs first and find customers later, Argentum locks in long-term customer commitments before financing and deploying capacity, a project-finance model that has Wall Street weighing a backlog some sources peg above $10B.

title: Zhipu weighs multibillion-dollar raise after 2,000% surge
blurb: Zhipu, the Chinese AI lab now trading as Knowledge Atlas Technology, is weighing a Hong Kong share sale to raise several billion dollars after its stock surged roughly 2,000% since January to a $128B market value. The rally followed Anthropic pulling its Fable 5 model under US export controls, a gap Zhipu filled with GLM-5.2, an open 744B-parameter model that ranked second on Arena's front-end coding leaderboard.

title: Alibaba sues the US government for adding it to list of firms linked to Chinese military
blurb: Alibaba sued the US government to be removed from the Pentagon's 1260H list of firms linked to the Chinese military, calling the designation "arbitrary and capricious" and without basis in fact or law. Inclusion bars the Defense Department from doing business with Alibaba and flags it as a risk for other firms, joining Baidu, which was added to the updated list earlier this month.

title: Zoox’s purpose-built robotaxi is getting a refresh
blurb: Zoox unveiled a refreshed version of its purpose-built, bidirectional robotaxi, updating the interior with monochrome aloe-green seating, stone-grey trim, and better-padded ergonomic seats based on rider feedback about firm, jarring rides. The Amazon-owned company runs free robotaxi services in San Francisco, Las Vegas, Austin, and Miami while awaiting federal approval to deploy a vehicle with no steering wheel or pedals.

title: AI Billionaires Score Win in $29 Million New York Proxy Fight
blurb: A super PAC backed by AI billionaires including Marc Andreessen and OpenAI president Greg Brockman spent over $8 million to defeat New York assemblyman Alex Bores in a congressional primary. Bores drew the industry's opposition after sponsoring state legislation to hold AI companies liable for harms caused by their products, making the $29 million race an early test of the sector's political spending power.

title: How space weather could bust the AI boom
blurb: A severe solar storm could cripple the strained US electric grid by damaging high-voltage transformers that take months or years to replace, threatening the AI data centers now drawing as much power as small cities. The author argues that big tech, with the most to lose, should fund better space weather forecasting, which gives grid operators advance warning to limit catastrophic damage.

title: This new tracking label could help solve cargo theft
blurb: Samsara released a business-card-sized sticky tracking label that uses Bluetooth and its existing device network to give real-time cargo location.

title: Chinese cybersecurity company 360 unveils new AI tools: Tulongfeng, which it claims is "China's version of Mythos", and Yitianzhen, to automate cyber defense (Eduardo Baptista/Reuters)
blurb: Chinese cybersecurity firm 360 introduced two AI security tools, Tulongfeng, which it positions as China's answer to the Mythos defense platform, and Yitianzhen, built to automate cyber defense. The release deepens China's push to build domestic AI security systems and reduce reliance on Western tooling, as both governments fold AI into national cyber strategy.

title: Modulate launches AI music detection as synthetic tracks flood streaming
blurb: Modulate launched AI Music Detection, an API that scores audio for AI-generated vocals and instrumentals and returns a verdict on the whole file. Built for streaming services, distributors, and rights holders, the tool hit 95% precision across 76 genres in internal testing against generators including Suno's 5.5 model, arriving as Suno raised $400M at a $5.4B valuation.

title: iPhone Ultra 3D-printed hinge problems reportedly solved, ready for September launch
blurb: Apple has resolved the durability and tolerance problems with the 3D-printed hinge on its first foldable, the iPhone Ultra, clearing the device for a September launch, according to a new supply-chain report in The Elec. Hinge modules from Taiwan's Shinjuxing and US-based Amphenol caused noise and defect-rate issues during testing, but the phone has now entered test production ahead of mass manufacturing.

title: OpenAI and Broadcom unveil Jalapeño, an LLM-optimized inference chip developed from design to manufacturing tape-out in nine months, aided by OpenAI's models (OpenAI)
blurb: OpenAI and Broadcom announced Jalapeño, an inference chip optimized for LLMs that went from design to manufacturing tape-out in nine months. OpenAI used its own models to accelerate the chip's development, marking the first custom silicon to emerge from the company's hardware partnership with Broadcom.

title: OpenAI unveils its first custom chip, built by Broadcom
blurb: OpenAI introduced Jalapeño, its first custom inference chip, designed with Broadcom to reduce the company's reliance on Nvidia GPUs. The chip is still in testing, but OpenAI says early results show significantly better performance-per-watt than current alternatives, with the design optimized for low-cost, real-time inference on workloads like coding models.

title: Embedding Forbidden Text in Spyware to Discourage AI Analysis
blurb: Malware authors are embedding fake system instructions and policy-triggering text about nuclear and biological weapons into spyware comment blocks to derail LLM-based code analysis. The header doesn't affect execution but can trigger refusals, context pollution, or premature classification in naive AI-first triage pipelines, though static tools like YARA rules, entropy checks, and AST parsing still detect the underlying payload.

title: OpenAI and Broadcom unveil LLM-optimized inference chip
blurb: OpenAI and Broadcom introduced Jalapeño, a custom chip built specifically for large language model inference. The companies designed it to improve performance, efficiency, and scale across AI systems, giving OpenAI more control over the compute stack that runs its models as it reduces reliance on third-party accelerators.

title: OpenAI, Broadcom Unveil Chip to Run Models Faster, Cheaper
blurb: OpenAI introduced its first custom AI chip, codenamed Jalapeno, developed with Broadcom and tailored to run the company's models more efficiently than off-the-shelf GPUs. OpenAI has received initial samples and is testing the accelerator on real workloads, with Broadcom CEO Hock Tan citing roughly 50% cost savings compared to typical AI GPUs.

title: OpenAI reveals its first AI processor: Jalapeño
blurb: OpenAI revealed Jalapeño, its first custom AI chip, an inference ASIC built with Broadcom to power ChatGPT and Codex requests while reducing its dependence on supply-constrained Nvidia GPUs. The company called it the first step in a multi-generation compute platform set for deployment by the end of 2026, with Broadcom's CEO claiming it matches the performance of Nvidia's Blackwell chips.

title: OpenAI, Broadcom Develop Custom Chip for AI Inference
blurb: OpenAI and Broadcom developed a custom AI inference chip called Jalapeño, built to improve efficiency and lower the cost of running OpenAI's models. The chip advances OpenAI's strategy to own more of the stack behind its products, reducing dependence on NVIDIA GPUs as inference demand scales.

title: Qualcomm to Buy Modular for $3.9 Billion to Help AI Push
blurb: Qualcomm announced it would acquire Modular for about $3.9 billion in stock to add AI software capabilities for its push into data centers. The deal is expected to close in the second half of 2026, with Qualcomm issuing Modular's owners 19.2 million shares based on its $204.13 closing price.

title: AI Is Designing Radio Chips That Humans Couldn’t Even Imagine
blurb: Princeton researchers applied reinforcement learning, inverse design, and diffusion models to generate radio-frequency integrated circuits (RFICs) from scratch, producing layouts that achieve record performance while cutting design time substantially. RFIC design has long remained an experience-driven craft that bottlenecks wireless progress, and the authors argue further gains will require large shared chip-design datasets and open ecosystems so models can learn universal electromagnetic and circuit behaviors.

title: Krea 2 Technical Report
blurb: Krea released Krea 2, a series of foundation image-generation models aimed at creative exploration rather than a single polished default aesthetic. The models use a diffusion transformer trained through a multi-stage pipeline of pretraining, midtraining, SFT, preference optimization, and RL, with architectural additions like grouped-query attention, sigmoid-gated attention, and Qwen3-VL text encoding.

title: OpenAI, Broadcom Develop AI Chip Called 'Jalapeno'
blurb: OpenAI and Broadcom developed a custom AI chip called Jalapeno, with OpenAI now testing early samples. The companies claim the chip can cut costs by 50%, part of OpenAI's broader push to reduce its dependence on NVIDIA hardware and control more of its own compute supply chain.

title: Qualcomm says it will acquire Modular, which builds a chip software platform and has a proprietary coding language, in a nearly $4B deal set to close in H2 2026 (Lauren Goode/Wired)
blurb: Qualcomm announced it will acquire Modular, the startup behind a chip software platform and the proprietary Mojo coding language, in a deal valued at nearly $4B and expected to close in the second half of 2026. The acquisition gives Qualcomm software tooling aimed at competing with NVIDIA's CUDA ecosystem across AI hardware.

title: GLM 5.2: why I’m replacing Opus in Claude Code with this new model
blurb: GLM 5.2, the open-weight coding model from Z.AI, was run through four real tasks inside a production Next.js codebase, including an architecture audit, a landing page redesign, and a 45-minute autonomous bug-hunting session pulling from Sentry and Vercel logs. The full run cost $3.36 for roughly 6 million tokens, with the author concluding the model is good enough to replace Opus in Claude Code.

title: Medical diagnosis AIs can be tricked into telling whose data trained them
blurb: Researchers showed that medical diagnosis AI models can be manipulated into revealing whose data was used to train them, exposing a privacy risk in clinical machine learning systems. The attack could let adversaries confirm whether a specific patient's records were part of a training set, undermining the anonymity protections that healthcare deployments depend on.

title: ‘Digit’ maker Agility Robotics to go public in $2.5B deal — here’s what the filings say about its finances
blurb: Agility Robotics agreed to go public through a merger with SPAC Churchill Capital Corp XI at a $2.5B valuation, becoming the first U.S. company dedicated solely to humanoid robots to reach Wall Street. The deal provides over $620M in cash, including roughly $200M from a Foxconn-led group, though filings withhold revenue and show the company remains unprofitable after burning about $100M in 2025.

title: SK hynix files to raise up to $29 billion in historic Nasdaq listing — all proceeds going to advanced AI memory fabs and EUV tool orders
blurb: SK hynix filed with regulators to raise up to $29.43B through an American depositary receipt listing on the Nasdaq Global Select Market, scheduled to debut July 10th. All proceeds are earmarked for AI memory capacity, funding its first Yongin cluster fab, a Cheongju HBM packaging plant, and a record $7.9B order for roughly 30 ASML EUV scanners through 2027.

title: London cops bring live facial recognition to West End
blurb: The Metropolitan Police deployed permanent live facial recognition cameras across London's West End, scanning passersby against watchlists in real time. The rollout marks a shift from mobile, event-based deployments to fixed infrastructure, intensifying civil liberties concerns over mass biometric surveillance in one of the city's busiest commercial districts.

title: Google tests voice dictation and Magic Pointer on Gemini desktop
blurb: Google is testing system-wide voice dictation and a Magic Pointer cursor-tracking feature in the macOS Gemini desktop app, alongside possible device-linking functionality. The additions would let Gemini capture spoken input and follow on-screen context across any application, extending the assistant beyond its current chat window into a system-level tool.

title: China black market Nvidia prices rocket in wake of smuggling crackdown and customs freeze — five-year-old A100 servers triple in price, now fetching up to $82,000
blurb: Black market prices for Nvidia AI hardware in China have spiked as a U.S. smuggling crackdown and a Chinese customs freeze cut off supply from both ends, with five-year-old A100 servers roughly tripling to as much as $82,000. Nvidia's DGX B300 now trades above $1.1 million versus a $400,000 U.S. retail price, and buyers are repurposing modified gaming GPUs while renting costs inside China match or exceed U.S. rates.

title: TSMC is reportedly hiking prices for 'all advanced nodes,' accounting for 74% of the company’s wafer business — Nvidia, AMD, Apple, Qualcomm, and others will face higher wafer costs
blurb: TSMC has reportedly told customers to prepare for price increases across all advanced nodes, extending hikes beyond 3nm to include 5nm and 7nm processes that together make up 74% of its wafer revenue. The increases reportedly fall in the 5% to 10% range and would raise wafer costs for major chip designers including Apple, Nvidia, AMD, Qualcomm, Broadcom, and MediaTek.

title: RunPod, which rents access to non-Nvidia servers, raised $100M led by Summit Partners at a $1B valuation, a source says up from $100M after its seed in 2024 (Stephanie Palazzolo/The Information)
blurb: RunPod, which rents access to non-Nvidia GPU servers, raised $100M led by Summit Partners at a $1B valuation. A source said the valuation is up from $100M after the company's 2024 seed round, reflecting demand for cheaper alternatives to Nvidia-based cloud compute.

title: Anthropic’s Claude Tag gives AI agents independent identities
blurb: Anthropic introduced an agent identity model for Claude Tag, giving Claude its own administrator-configured identity, permissions, and tool access tied to a workspace or channel rather than individual user credentials. The approach isolates private channels under separate identities, lets admins scope access per channel and revoke everything by disabling one identity, and keeps Claude from exposing private documents through shared channels.

title: Qualcomm inks deal for AI startup Modular to bolster software stack, data center build-out
blurb: Qualcomm announced it would acquire infrastructure startup Modular, reportedly for around $3.92B, to strengthen its AI software stack and data center push. The deal targets the inference market and is expected to close in the second half of 2026, positioning Qualcomm to help enterprises run AI more efficiently as token costs climb.

title: Google’s New $100 Speaker Makes a Weak Case for Gemini in the Home
blurb: Google released the Google Home Speaker, a $100 device launching June 25 that runs Gemini for always-on voice interaction and marks Alphabet's first new speaker in six years. The review found the Gemini integration capable but the audio quality underwhelming, making a weak case for the device over earlier Google Assistant hardware.

title: AI coding agents could soon cost more than the developers using them
blurb: AI coding agents are on track to cost more per month than the salaries of the developers who use them, as token consumption from autonomous, long-running tasks climbs. The piece argues that current per-seat and subscription pricing masks the true expense, and that as agents take on more work, those costs will surface and keep rising.

title: MGX Sets Up $50 Billion AI Fund
blurb: MGX, the Abu Dhabi investment firm, raised close to $50B from regional and global investors for one of the largest dedicated AI investment vehicles to date. The fund draws on both local sovereign capital and outside backers to fund AI infrastructure and companies as Gulf states expand their positions in the sector.

title: Chinese Supercomputer Overtakes U.S. as World’s Fastest
blurb: China's LineShine system took the No. 1 spot on the latest Top500 supercomputer ranking released this week, overtaking U.S. machines as the world's fastest. The result marks a shift in the high-performance computing race that underpins AI training and large-scale scientific work, reversing the lead the U.S. had held atop the list.

title: Metas Ex-KI-Chef sammelt über eine Milliarde Dollar für neues Startup ein
blurb: Yann LeCun's new Paris-based startup AMI Labs raised $1.03B in seed funding from investors including Bezos Expeditions, Greycroft, and HV Capital, with Alex LeBrun joining as CEO. The lab, which LeCun announced after leaving Meta in November, will focus on world models, AI systems built to understand and reflect how the physical world works.

title: The OpenAI API everyone copied isn't the one OpenAI recommends
blurb: This post argues that the "OpenAI-compatible" badge stamped on tools like Ollama, vLLM, and LM Studio refers to the older Chat Completions API, not the Responses API that OpenAI introduced in March 2025 and now recommends for new projects. The author shows that the format the industry cloned into a de facto standard is the one OpenAI is steering developers away from, so "compatible" almost always means the legacy spec rather than the current direction.

title: Meta, Microsoft Lead $850 Billion Boom in Data Center Leases
blurb: Meta and Microsoft each committed tens of billions of dollars in additional data center leases last quarter, pushing total future lease obligations among the largest cloud providers past $850 billion. The commitments have climbed steadily over the past year as tech giants race to expand their server fleets for AI workloads, according to a Bloomberg analysis of regulatory filings.

title: Zhipu Said to Weigh Multibillion-Dollar Share Sale in Hong Kong After 2,000% Gain
blurb: Zhipu is considering a share sale in Hong Kong to raise several billion US dollars, working with advisers on a placement that could happen as soon as next month. The Chinese AI model maker has gained about 2,000% since its January listing, and a six-month IPO lock-up expires on July 8.

title: SK Hynix Seeks to Raise $29 Billion in US Listing to Meet AI Chip Demand
blurb: SK Hynix filed to raise $29.4B in a US listing, with trading expected to begin July 10, in a deal that could rank among the five largest share sales ever. The chipmaker, a key supplier of high-bandwidth memory for AI accelerators, is tapping public markets to fund capacity expansion as demand for AI chips climbs.

title: US government reportedly urging Meta to share its AI models
blurb: The US government is reportedly urging Meta to submit its AI models for safety and security evaluation, making it the only major AI developer that hasn't voluntarily turned its models over for review. OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, xAI, and Microsoft have already agreed to give the Center for AI Standards and Innovation early access, following a June 2 executive order that asks companies for up to 30 days of pre-release review.

title: A Princeton grad’s $30M AI detector is selling to Superhuman
blurb: Superhuman, the productivity firm formerly known as Grammarly, agreed to acquire GPTZero, the AI-writing detector founded by Edward Tian as a Princeton thesis project in 2023. GPTZero reached 19 million registered users and $30M in annual recurring revenue on just $13.5M in funding, and its 30-person team will now build what Superhuman calls an "authenticity layer.

title: NSA Loses Anthropic Mythos Access After June Export-Control Order
blurb: The NSA lost access to Anthropic's Mythos 5 cybersecurity model after the Trump administration's June export-control directive forced Anthropic to suspend its most advanced models for all foreign nationals, including noncitizen employees, The New York Times reported. Officials clarified that earlier claims of Mythos breaking into classified systems in hours referred to a controlled red-team exercise, not an outside breach.

title: Q&A with AWS CEO Matt Garman on the parallels between early AWS and AI, Quick, AI coding, Amazon's $200B capex in 2026, entry-level jobs changing, and more (Casey Newton/Platformer)
blurb: In a Platformer Q&A, AWS CEO Matt Garman drew parallels between the early days of AWS and the current AI buildout, while addressing AI coding, entry-level job shifts, and Amazon's plans for roughly $200B in capital expenditure in 2026. He argued that today's infrastructure race echoes cloud computing's adoption curve, with demand growth justifying the spend.

title: Beyond the $7.8B in deals: Why Wall Street is suddenly watching Argentum AI
blurb: Argentum AI, an infrastructure startup led by Andrew Sobko and backed by Supermicro, signed roughly $7.8B in agreements to deploy about 47,000 NVIDIA GB300 GPUs across a 300-megawatt datacenter buildout. Industry sources suggest the company's contracted backlog may exceed $10B, pointing to a project-finance model that secures long-term customer commitments before deploying infrastructure rather than buying GPUs first.

title: Zhipu weighs multibillion-dollar raise after 2,000% surge
blurb: Zhipu, now trading as Knowledge Atlas Technology, is weighing a Hong Kong share sale to raise several billion dollars after its stock rose roughly 2,000% since January, pushing its market value above HK$1 trillion. The placement could come as soon as next month once a six-month IPO lock-up expires on July 8, with the company also aiming to issue shares in Shanghai.

title: Coval raises $28M as enterprises push voice agents into production
blurb: Coval raised $28M to expand its testing platform for AI voice agents, which runs simulations, tracks live performance, and labels data so teams can evaluate agents before and after launch. Founded in 2024 by former Waymo evaluation engineer Brooke Hopkins, Coval now counts more than 60 customers including Zoom and Deepgram, and says its probabilistic evaluations cut manual QA work by up to 30 times.

title: Chinese cybersecurity company 360 unveils new AI tools: Tulongfeng, which it claims is "China's version of Mythos", and Yitianzhen, to automate cyber defense (Eduardo Baptista/Reuters)
blurb: Chinese cybersecurity firm 360 released two AI tools, Tulongfeng, which it describes as China's version of Mythos, and Yitianzhen, built to automate cyber defense. The company is positioning the tools to handle threat detection and response with less human intervention, part of a broader push by Chinese vendors into agent-driven security.

title: Modulate launches AI music detection as synthetic tracks flood streaming
blurb: Modulate released AI Music Detection, an API that flags AI-generated music directly from the audio by scoring the likelihood that a clip contains AI vocals or AI instrumentals. The system runs three models in ensemble, separating vocal and instrumental analysis to catch hybrid tracks, and reached 95% precision across 76 genres in internal testing against generators including Suno's 5.5 model.

title: OpenAI, Broadcom Develop AI Chip Called 'Jalapeno'
blurb: OpenAI and Broadcom have developed a custom AI chip called Jalapeno, with OpenAI now testing samples and the companies claiming it can cut costs by half. The move pushes OpenAI further off its dependence on Nvidia and into owning its own silicon, where the savings compound at the scale it runs inference. Custom chips are becoming the default play for any lab spending billions on compute, and the gap between firms that control their hardware stack and those renting it is about to widen.

title: OpenAI reveals its first AI processor: Jalapeño
blurb: OpenAI revealed Jalapeño, its first custom inference chip, built with Broadcom and aimed at cutting the company's dependence on scarce Nvidia GPUs. Broadcom's CEO says it matches Nvidia's Blackwell and Google's TPUs on performance, with early testing showing substantially better performance per watt, and OpenAI expects to deploy it by the end of 2026. The frontier labs are now vertically integrating down to silicon, which over time loosens Nvidia's pricing grip and reshapes the compute capex math that every AI startup ultimately pays for.

title: OpenAI, Broadcom Unveil Chip to Run Models Faster, Cheaper
blurb: OpenAI got its first samples of Jalapeno, a custom AI chip built with Broadcom, and is now testing how it runs the company's own workloads. Early results show roughly 50% cost savings versus the GPUs everyone else rents from Nvidia, according to Broadcom CEO Hock Tan. The frontier labs are moving down the stack into their own silicon, and if the savings hold at scale, OpenAI's cost-per-token advantage becomes a moat that anyone still paying GPU rates cannot match.

title: OpenAI unveils its first custom chip, built by Broadcom
blurb: OpenAI released Jalapeño, its first custom inference chip, co-designed with Broadcom and partly developed with OpenAI's own models. The play is margin: the chip targets the cost of running real-time models like Codex, where early tests show better performance-per-watt than current alternatives, while pre-training likely stays on Nvidia. It puts OpenAI on the same path as Google and Amazon, owning every layer from chip to product so inference economics stop depending on a single supplier. For startups built on OpenAI's API, cheaper inference upstream is the lever that eventually moves their own unit costs.

title: OpenAI and Broadcom unveil Jalapeño, an LLM-optimized inference chip developed from design to manufacturing tape-out in nine months, aided by OpenAI's models (OpenAI)
blurb: OpenAI and Broadcom built Jalapeño, an inference chip tuned for LLMs, from first design to manufacturing tape-out in nine months, with OpenAI's own models helping compress a process that normally runs years. The loop is closing on itself: the frontier labs are now using AI to design the silicon that runs AI, cutting both their Nvidia dependence and their cost per token at once. The signal for everyone downstream is that inference pricing is set increasingly by labs that own their hardware, and that price is heading one direction.

title: OpenAI, Broadcom Develop Custom Chip for AI Inference
blurb: OpenAI is co-designing a custom inference chip with Broadcom, called Jalapeño, built to cut the cost and improve the efficiency of running its models. The move pushes OpenAI further into owning the full stack beneath its products, from models down to the silicon they run on. For a company whose biggest constraint is compute economics, controlling the inference hardware is how it stops paying Nvidia's margins and starts setting its own cost curve.

title: Qualcomm to Buy Modular for $3.9 Billion to Help AI Push
blurb: Qualcomm is acquiring Modular for about $3.9 billion in stock, adding AI software to support its move into the data center market. The deal hands Modular's owners 19.2 million shares and is set to close in the second half of 2026. Modular's pitch was always portable inference that runs across different chips, so the buyer is a mobile-chip company trying to break Nvidia's grip on AI infrastructure by pairing its silicon with a software layer that already abstracts away the hardware underneath.

title: Adobe Just Deferred a Big Annual Price Increase. It’s the First Big Crack in B2B Pricing Power Since 2022.
blurb: Adobe just deferred roughly $500M ARR of Creative Cloud price increases it had already planned for the back half of FY2026, breaking a decade-long habit of raising prices like clockwork. For four years the B2B playbook was identical: hike every 12 to 18 months, bolt on an AI SKU, push net revenue retention above 110%, and customers paid because they had nowhere to go. When one of software's most pricing-powerful companies voluntarily walks away from a planned hike, the signal isn't generosity. It's the first real crack in B2B pricing power since 2022.

title: Qualcomm says it will acquire Modular, which builds a chip software platform and has a proprietary coding language, in a nearly $4B deal set to close in H2 2026 (Lauren Goode/Wired)
blurb: Qualcomm agreed to acquire Modular, the AI chip-software startup behind the Mojo language, for nearly $4B, with the deal expected to close in H2 2026. The bet is on software, not silicon: Modular's platform abstracts away the hardware so developers can run AI workloads without rewriting for each chip, which is the layer Nvidia's CUDA has locked up for years. Qualcomm is paying a steep price to buy a credible path into the AI compute stack rather than build one, a sign of how much the data-center fight now turns on the developer toolchain.

title: SK hynix files to raise up to $29 billion in historic Nasdaq listing — all proceeds going to advanced AI memory fabs and EUV tool orders
blurb: SK hynix filed to raise up to $29.43B through a Nasdaq ADR listing on July 10th, one of the largest such sales ever, with every dollar earmarked for AI memory fabs and EUV scanner orders. None of it eases the current shortage: the Yongin Y1 fab and Cheongju packaging plant don't reach volume output until 2027, and chairman Chey Tae-won expects AI demand to keep supply tight through 2030. SK hynix already holds about 57% of the HBM market and 32% of global DRAM, and it just placed a record $7.9B order with ASML for roughly 30 scanners.

title: ‘Digit’ maker Agility Robotics to go public in $2.5B deal — here’s what the filings say about its finances
blurb: Agility Robotics is taking its Digit humanoid public through a $2.5B SPAC merger with Churchill Capital Corp XI, becoming the first US-listed company built solely around humanoid robots. The filings show why the timing matters more than the numbers: Agility doesn't disclose revenue, remains unprofitable, burned roughly $100M in cash last year, and saw operating expenses climb to about $111M from $71M. Its headline $300M in "committed" Digit v5 orders comes with fine print that it isn't current revenue and hinges on a single undisclosed customer. The SPAC reload is funding humanoid bets long before the unit economics are proven.

title: Founding a company in Germany: €9600, 152 days and I still can't send an invoice
blurb: A founder spent more than €9,600 and 152 days incorporating his second company in Germany, and he still can't send a single invoice. The notary, two courts, a law firm, a tax firm, and the software vendors all billed him on time, while the one VAT ID he needs for reverse-charge invoicing to foreign clients is still coming by post. Everyone in the chain can invoice him; he is the only one who can't invoice anyone.

title: Slate Auto’s radically simple electric truck starts at $24,950
blurb: Slate Auto priced its bare-bones electric truck at $24,950, about half the average cost of a new car in the US and below the Chevy Bolt and Nissan Leaf. The base model has hand-crank windows, no infotainment, no paint options, and a single gray composite finish, with buyers converting the two-seater pickup into a five-seat SUV themselves. The bet is that stripping out cost centers like factory paint shops and dealerships opens the lowest end of the market, where gas options are thin and electric ones thinner.

title: RunPod, which rents access to non-Nvidia servers, raised $100M led by Summit Partners at a $1B valuation, a source says up from $100M after its seed in 2024 (Stephanie Palazzolo/The Information)
blurb: RunPod raised $100M led by Summit Partners at a $1B valuation, up from a $100M valuation after its 2024 seed, a tenfold jump in under two years. The company rents GPU compute on non-Nvidia hardware, betting that founders training and serving models care more about price and availability than which chip is underneath. The signal for operators is that the compute bottleneck is pushing capital toward anyone who can offer cheaper alternatives to Nvidia, and that demand is now strong enough to mint a unicorn out of arbitraging it.

title: Qualcomm inks deal for AI startup Modular to bolster software stack, data center build-out
blurb: Qualcomm agreed to buy AI infrastructure startup Modular for a reported $3.92B to strengthen its software stack and push into the data center inference market. The buy is a bet on horizontal, developer-friendly platforms that run AI across different compute, positioning Qualcomm against Nvidia's grip on inference. The timing tracks a real operator pain point: enterprises are scrambling to cut AI spend as token costs eat into budgets, and the labs and chipmakers are racing to own the layer that makes inference cheaper to run.

title: Walmart buys Vibe.co for $1.4bn to chase Amazon in ads
blurb: Walmart agreed to buy Vibe.co, a self-serve connected-TV ad platform, for a reported $1.4bn, more than three times the $410m valuation investors gave it nine months ago. The number that matters is the multiple: about 12.6 times revenue, against roughly one times for listed rival MNTN. Walmart isn't paying for current sales, it's paying to own a piece of the plumbing behind retail media and close the gap with Amazon, where high-margin ad revenue is pure profit bolted onto a thin-margin grocery business.

title: Memory Chipmaker SK Hynix's $29 Billion US Listing Seizes on AI Demand
blurb: SK Hynix is seeking $29.4 billion in a US listing, tapping investor appetite for memory-chip stocks even after a selloff rattled the group this week. The offering follows an 850% run in the Seoul-traded stock over the past 12 months, pushing the company's market value above $1 trillion. Memory has become the leveraged play on AI buildout, but sentiment swings in both directions sharply, and a listing this size signals the company wants to lock in dollar capital while the demand premium holds.

title: NYC-based Taktile, which helps fintechs build automated decision-making workflows, raised a $110M Series C led by Goldman Sachs, and plans a São Paulo office (Camila Grigera Naón/Fortune)
blurb: Taktile raised a $110M Series C led by Goldman Sachs, valuing the platform that lets fintechs build and ship automated decision-making workflows, with a new São Paulo office on the way. Goldman leading the round signals that risk and underwriting logic is moving out of hand-coded backends and into configurable decision engines banks and lenders can iterate on directly. The São Paulo push points where the next demand sits, as Latin American fintechs scale credit and fraud operations and need the same automated decisioning the incumbents are now buying.

title: MGX Sets Up $50 Billion AI Fund
blurb: Abu Dhabi's MGX has raised close to $50 billion for a dedicated AI investment vehicle, one of the largest ever assembled, drawing from both regional and global investors. The fund signals that sovereign capital, not Silicon Valley venture, is now setting the pace for AI infrastructure and model bets at the top of the stack. For founders, it means the biggest checks increasingly come from state-backed pools with long horizons and geopolitical agendas, which reshapes who you raise from and what strings come attached.

title: Agility Robotics goes public at a $2.5B valuation, and its humanoid robots are already working in warehouses
blurb: Agility Robotics is going public via a SPAC merger with Churchill Capital Corp XI at a $2.5B valuation, raising more than $620M in gross proceeds. What separates it from the rest of the humanoid field is a commercial track record: its Digit robots already run on live production floors at Schaeffler, GXO, Toyota, and Mercado Libre, logging more than 65,000 operating hours across nine customer facilities. Foxconn, the iPhone manufacturer, is leading the PIPE, a signal that humanoid robotics is moving from research demos into a scale-manufacturing phase.

title: South Korea’s biggest chipmaker SK Hynix plans to raise $29 billion via Nasdaq listing as soon as July 10
blurb: SK Hynix, now South Korea's most valuable company, plans to raise around $29 billion on the Nasdaq via American depositary receipts, with trading tentatively starting July 10. The chipmaker holds roughly 60% of the high-bandwidth memory market that AI systems are starving for, and its shares have soared over 280% this year to push its market cap past $1 trillion. The listing plants it in the US just as it builds a $4 billion packaging plant in Indiana and a massive new fab cluster at home, putting the HBM supply that every AI buildout depends on closer to American capital.

title: The VC that turned a $750M Anthropic deal into a $14B stake just raised $3B to do it again
blurb: Menlo Ventures raised $3 billion across two AI funds, its largest haul in 50 years, on the strength of an Anthropic stake now worth roughly $14 billion off about $1 billion invested. The firm backed Anthropic in 2023 when it was pre-product and pre-revenue, then led its $750M Series D through an unconventional special-purpose vehicle. One foundation-model bet now bankrolls a full-stack AI portfolio spanning Lovable, Suno, and Skild AI, which is the playbook every AI-era fund is now trying to copy.

title: Meta, Microsoft Lead $850 Billion Boom in Data Center Leases
blurb: Future data center lease commitments among the largest cloud companies have crossed $850 billion, with Meta and Microsoft each adding tens of billions in their latest quarters. The number has climbed steadily over the past year as the hyperscalers race to expand their server fleets for AI, and these are contractual obligations on the books, not aspirational capex slides. For founders building on their infrastructure, the buildout sets the floor under compute pricing and signals that the capacity arms race is locking in years before the demand to justify it is proven.

title: Metas Ex-KI-Chef sammelt über eine Milliarde Dollar für neues Startup ein
blurb: Yann LeCun raised $1.03B in seed funding for AMI Labs, the Paris startup he founded after leaving Meta, with backing from Bezos Expeditions, Greycroft, Cathay Innovation, HV Capital, and Hiro Capital. The bet is on world models, AI systems that learn how the physical world actually works rather than predicting the next token, and LeCun is staffing it across Paris, New York, Montreal, and Singapore. It is one of the few frontier labs positioned as neither Chinese nor American, with former Facebook researcher Alex LeBrun installed as CEO and a team that treats this as a long-horizon science project, not a product race.

title: PayPal Put Agentforce on 8,000 Leads a Month No Human Was Going to Call. Conversions Jumped 50%.
blurb: PayPal pointed an Agentforce SDR agent at the 8,000 merchants a month its 200-rep org was never going to call, the ones who stalled partway through onboarding, and meeting conversion is now running about 50% higher than reps managed alone. The agent runs a 10-nudge cadence on every lead and books the meeting, then hands the rep a qualified prospect with full context loaded. The lesson for operators is to stop waiting on clean CRM data and aim agents at the leads you already drop, where anything beats zero coverage.

title: Zhipu Said to Weigh Multibillion-Dollar Share Sale in Hong Kong After 2,000% Gain
blurb: Chinese AI model maker Zhipu is weighing a share sale to raise several billion dollars in Hong Kong, as soon as next month, after its stock climbed about 2,000% since listing in January. A six-month IPO lock-up expires July 8, clearing the way for the placement. The run reflects how aggressively public markets are repricing China's frontier labs, and gives Zhipu a war chest to fund compute and model training while Western capital largely stays on the sidelines.

title: AI coding agents could soon cost more than the developers using them
blurb: AI coding agents are on track to cost more per month than the salaries of the developers running them, as token-heavy autonomous workflows rack up usage faster than any per-seat license ever did. A single Codex-style agent left running can burn through compute continuously, and recent reports show clumsy logging alone quietly writing data and costing millions. The implication is that the old per-developer pricing math breaks down, and the constraint on engineering output shifts from headcount to how tightly you govern agent spend.

title: A Princeton grad’s $30M AI detector is selling to Superhuman
blurb: Superhuman, the productivity firm formerly called Grammarly, acquired GPTZero, the startup best known for catching AI-written text, in an undisclosed deal that values GPTZero above $88M by PitchBook's count. The pairing is the point: Superhuman sells AI writing tools, and as machine-made text floods the web, proving something is human is becoming its own category, what Superhuman calls an "authenticity layer." Founder Edward Tian built GPTZero as a Princeton senior in 2023 and grew it to 19 million users and $30M in ARR on just $13.5M raised.

title: SpaceX Starfall Demo Provides Validation for Reentry Startups
blurb: SpaceX launched a demo of its Starfall reentry capsule, signaling that an industry giant now sees money in returning cargo from orbit. Founders at the dozen-plus reentry startups already in the market read the move as validation, not a threat: when SpaceX enters a space, it confirms the demand signal is real. The specs back that reading, since Starfall carries about 1 metric ton and runs 3x the size of Varda's capsules but can't maneuver in orbit or guide itself to a precise landing, leaving room for rivals to own the niches it doesn't.

title: Hyperscalers Ditch Canada Investor Calls and Buyers Don’t Care
blurb: Amazon and Alphabet raised C$22.5 billion ($15.8 billion) in their first Canadian maple bond issues to fund AI buildouts, and fund managers absorbed the debt without the usual investor calls. Demand was strong enough that buyers are now waiting on Meta to follow with its own first issue, which it has shown no need to do. The signal for founders is that hyperscaler AI capex now commands so much trust that the largest tech borrowers can skip the courtship and still clear billions in a single sitting.

title: Slate Auto says each $24,950 electric pickup truck will be profitable as it aims to be cash-flow positive next year
blurb: Slate Auto says every one of its $24,950 electric pickups will ship gross-margin positive, with CEO Peter Faricy targeting positive free cash flow and EBITDA by 2027. The math leans on a stripped-down truck (optional speakers, crank windows, 205-mile range) and a break-even point of roughly 80,000 vehicles a year, just over half its planned 150,000-unit capacity in Warsaw, Indiana. That bet runs against the recent EV-startup record, where Lordstown and Fisker went bankrupt and Rivian and Lucid posted billions in losses before their latest layoffs.

title: Main Capital raised the Netherlands’ biggest-ever fund on boring software
blurb: Main Capital Partners, a Hague-based enterprise software investor, just closed €5.25bn across two oversubscribed funds, the largest private equity buyout raise in Dutch history, betting on the unglamorous software that public markets are fleeing. The iShares software ETF has fallen about 30% from its September 2025 peak on fears that AI will hollow out enterprise software, yet Main's backers asked for no reassurance. Over 23 years buying hospital scheduling and municipal tax systems, the firm has logged a loss rate under 0.5% and 38 exits at 4.7x, and that track record did the persuading.

title: Hadrian’s reported $7.5bn round and the physical-AI rush
blurb: Bloomberg reports Hadrian, a startup that builds AI-run factories for aerospace and defense parts, is in talks to raise up to $1bn at a $7.5bn valuation, more than quadrupling the $1.6bn it was worth in January. Hadrian calls the figure "inaccurate," but the exact number matters less than what a five-month markup that steep signals: capital is rushing into physical AI and US reindustrialisation at the same time. When a whole theme runs this hot, even a disputed valuation tells founders where the money wants to go next.

title: The US FDA drops an enforcement complaint against Whoop over its blood pressure tracking tool, reversing a July 2025 warning letter; Whoop is modifying the tool (Samantha Kelly/Bloomberg)
blurb: The FDA dropped its enforcement complaint against Whoop over its blood pressure feature, reversing the warning letter it sent in July 2025, and Whoop is reworking the tool in exchange. The reversal signals a softer regulatory line on consumer wearables that edge into medical-device territory, the gray zone where most health hardware now competes. For any founder building a device that measures something a doctor usually measures, the takeaway is that the line between wellness feature and regulated diagnostic is negotiable, but you still pay for it in product changes.

title: Q&A with AWS CEO Matt Garman on the parallels between early AWS and AI, Quick, AI coding, Amazon's $200B capex in 2026, entry-level jobs changing, and more (Casey Newton/Platformer)
blurb: AWS CEO Matt Garman argues the AI buildout rhymes with early cloud, where the hard part was never the demand but committing capital years ahead of it. Amazon plans roughly $200B in capex in 2026, betting that inference demand keeps compounding the way EC2 did once developers stopped asking permission to spin up servers. He also expects entry-level work to shift toward judgment over rote execution, which reshapes how founders should think about their first ten hires.

title: Amazon CEO Ramps Up Fast Commerce Push in Maiden India Trip
blurb: Amazon is multiplying the Indian cities and towns covered by Amazon Now, its minutes-delivery service, scaling from a handful of locations last year to more than 300 cities and towns now. Andy Jassy announced the push on his first India trip as CEO, a direct play for a growth market where local quick-commerce leaders already set the pace. The pattern matters because global incumbents are conceding that you can't win India's retail spend with standard logistics, you have to match the local speed bar that rivals like Blinkit and Zepto established first.

title: Waymo Charging Partner Terawatt Raises Up to $300 Million in Debt
blurb: Terawatt Infrastructure, an EV charging operator that counts Waymo among its customers, raised as much as $300M in debt to fund acquisition and buildout of charging sites in the US and abroad. The facility is a five-year senior secured line led by RBC Capital Markets, starting at $150M with an option to double it, alongside Sumitomo Mitsui and UBS. The structure is the signal: the buildout under autonomous and electric fleets is now being financed with bank debt rather than venture equity, which is how a layer graduates from speculative bet to infrastructure.

title: Tech Stocks Lead Bounce After $1.3 Trillion Rout on Nasdaq 100
blurb: Tech stocks led a rebound after Tuesday's selloff wiped roughly $1.3 trillion off the Nasdaq 100, with the S&P 500 up 0.7% and the Dow up 0.9%. The bounce came as falling crude oil and weak housing data pushed traders to bet the Fed has less reason to hike. The swing shows how tightly AI-driven equities now track macro signals, and how fast sentiment reverses, so founders raising or timing liquidity events are operating in a market that can shed and recover a trillion dollars in two sessions.

title: Beyond the $7.8B in deals: Why Wall Street is suddenly watching Argentum AI
blurb: Argentum AI, a startup less than a year old, signed roughly $7.8B in agreements to deploy about 47,000 Nvidia GB300 GPUs across a 300-megawatt datacenter buildout, with sources putting its real backlog above $10B. What has Wall Street watching isn't the GPU count but the model: Argentum locks in long-term customer commitments first, then finances and deploys the infrastructure to fulfill them. That inverts the past two years of neocloud strategy, where everyone raced to buy GPUs first and find customers later, and it looks more like a project-finance platform than an AI startup.

title: Zhipu weighs multibillion-dollar raise after 2,000% surge
blurb: Zhipu's shares are up roughly 2,000% since January, pushing the lab now trading as Knowledge Atlas Technology above $128B, and it is weighing a Hong Kong placement to raise several billion dollars as soon as next month. The rally prices in more than a hot Chinese AI stock, because Anthropic pulled Fable 5 and Mythos 5 worldwide on June 12 after US export controls, and Zhipu shipped a fully open GLM-5.2 the next day that reached second on Arena's coding leaderboard. The open question is whether a deeply unprofitable lab can convert a geopolitical accident into a durable business.

title: SK Hynix sets a $29 billion US listing that could top every ADR deal ever done
blurb: SK Hynix filed to raise up to $29B through a Nasdaq ADR listing slated for July 10, which at the top of its range would be the largest ADR deal ever, ahead of Alibaba's $21.8B in 2014. The entire raise funds capacity, not shareholders or debt, with the money earmarked for fabs in Yongin and Cheongju and EUV scanners to feed the high-bandwidth memory that sits beside Nvidia's accelerators. SK Hynix already supplies an estimated 60% to 70% of the HBM4 going into Nvidia's Vera Rubin platform and just passed Samsung as Korea's most valuable listed company.

title: Hightouch : pourquoi cette startup a fait une offre à 1,2 milliard de dollars à Publicis
